preamble: In Haudricourt's (1954) model of Tonogenesis, the elision of a final glottal stop [ʔ] is the primary driver for the development of a rising Pitch Contour. This Laryngeal Coda originally caused a rise in the fundamental frequency of the preceding vowel; when the stop was lost, this pitch contrast was phonemicized as a tone.

examples.note: The loss of final glottal stop in Mon-Khmer roots led to the development of the hỏi/ngã tones (rising) in Vietnamese.
The 'Rising' category (Shàng sheng) in Middle Chinese is widely reconstructed as having a glottal coda [ʔ] which was subsequently lost.
In Tai-Kadai, final glottal features interacted with vowel length and initial voicing to produce distinct tonal contours during the Register Split.

sources: Haudricourt, A. G. (1954). De l'origine des tons en viêtnamien.
Matisoff, J. A. (1973). Tonogenesis in Southeast Asia.
